title: Cannot use environment variable in --entrypoint
body:
### Issue Description

When creating a container, I am willing to configure an entrypoint using `--entrypoint` where one of the arguments is defined in an environment variable. (The goal is to be able to `commit` this container into a proper image in a later step).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```console
podman container run --rm \
                     -e mymessage=test \
                     --entrypoint '["echo","${mymessage}"]' \
                     docker.io/alpine
```

### Describe the results you received

It prints

```
${mymessage}
```

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect this command to print  : 

```
test
```

comments.body:
If you use `sh` it is possible to expand the environment variable

```
$ podman container run --rm -e mymessage=test --entrypoint '["sh","-c","echo ${mymessage}"]' docker.io/alpine
test
```
This is working as expected, just as @eriksjolund described. Thanks! The variable is propagated correctly, so I'm closing this as it's not a bug.

Ah I see, thank you!

What surprises me is that if I specify the entrypoint using `--change` when doing a `commit`, it works as expected:

```console
$ podman container create --name alpine_template docker.io/alpine
$ podman container commit --change  'ENTRYPOINT echo ${mymessage}' alpine_template
$ podman container run --rm --env mymessage=test localhost/my_echo_image:latest 
test
```

Does it mean that `ENTRYPOINT` implicitly calls `sh`, which is able to expand the variable?
